title: Why Do Some Promises Stick and Others Don't?

situation.content:
You tell yourself you'll wake up at 5am tomorrow. You set an alarm. Tomorrow comes and you hit snooze. The promise evaporated because it cost nothing to break.

Or: a CEO promises their board they'll hit quarterly targets. Quarter ends, targets missed, the CEO offers explanations. The promise meant nothing because there was no structural consequence for breaking it.

Or: two countries sign a peace treaty. Within years, one side violates it. The paper promise carried no weight because the cost of breaking it was less than the benefit of cheating.

Promises fail when breaking them is cheap. They stick when breaking them is catastrophically expensive. The question isn't whether you mean your promise — it's whether the structure makes breaking it irrational.

story.content:
In February 1519, Hernán Cortés landed on the coast of Mexico with roughly 600 men, 16 horses, and a few cannons. His mission: conquer the Aztec empire — a civilization of millions with a standing army that dwarfed his force by a factor of a thousand.

His men knew the odds. Many had served on previous failed expeditions. The rational move was obvious: probe the coast, take what gold you can, and retreat to the ships if things go badly. Always keep your exit option.

Cortés understood that this rational exit option was precisely what would defeat them. As long as retreat was possible, his men would fight with one eye on the shore. Every difficult moment would trigger the calculation: "Is this worth dying for when the ships are right there?" And the answer, rationally, would always be no.

So Cortés gave the order to scuttle the ships.

The historical record debates whether they were burned, sunk, or dismantled — but the effect was identical. The exit option was destroyed. Permanently. Publicly. Irreversibly.

Now the calculation changed completely. With no ships, the options narrowed to two: conquer or die. And if you're going to die anyway if you fail, you might as well fight with absolute commitment. The destroyed ships didn't add any military capability. They added *credibility* — to his own men (who could no longer waver) and to the Aztecs (who could see that these invaders had nowhere to go and would fight to the death).

This is a commitment device: a deliberate destruction of future options that makes a promise credible by making it impossible to break.

Three thousand years earlier, Homer described the same logic from the inside. Odysseus, approaching the island of the Sirens — whose song drove sailors to crash their ships on rocks — gave an order: tie me to the mast. Fill your ears with wax. No matter what I say, no matter how I beg, do not untie me.

Odysseus didn't trust his future self. He knew that in the moment of temptation, his present rationality would evaporate. So his present self bound his future self — creating a physical constraint that his future self couldn't override regardless of desire.

This is the internal version of Cortés's logic. Cortés burned ships to commit others. Odysseus tied himself to commit against himself. Both understood the same principle: a promise that relies on willpower alone is not a promise — it's a wish. A promise that's structurally impossible to break is credible.

Commitment devices work because they change the payoff structure. Before Cortés burns the ships, defection (retreat) is cheap and tempting. After, defection is physically impossible. The game is transformed from one where breaking the commitment is rational to one where it's not even an option.

This is why mere verbal promises fail in game theory. "I promise I won't retreat" carries no weight if the ships are right there. The words cost nothing. The action of destroying them costs everything — and that's why it's believed.
story.title: The Story

contrast.content:
Marriage, in its structural design (not its romantic meaning), is one of history's most elegant commitment devices.

Consider what it does mechanically: two people who want to remain committed to each other publicly, legally, financially, and socially destroy their exit option. Divorce is expensive, legally complex, socially costly, and emotionally devastating. The relationship could be ended — but the cost of ending it is so high that it changes the payoff structure of every small decision within it.

This isn't a romantic observation. It's a structural one. A cohabiting couple who loves each other just as deeply faces a fundamentally different game-theoretic situation: the exit cost is low. Move out, split the Netflix account, update your address. Because exit is cheap, every conflict carries the implicit threat of departure. And because that threat exists, both parties invest less — why pour your soul into something that could evaporate with a one-month notice?

Marriage flips this. By making departure catastrophically expensive, it makes investment rational. If you can't easily leave, you might as well make it good. The commitment device changes not just the likelihood of staying, but the quality of effort within staying.

Research on relationship investment (Rusbult's Investment Model, 1980) confirms this: relationship satisfaction is predicted not just by rewards and alternatives, but by *investment size*. The more you've sunk in (publicly, financially, structurally), the more you invest going forward — because the commitment makes the investment safe.

The counterintuitive insight: constraints create freedom. By destroying the option to leave cheaply, marriage creates the psychological safety to be fully vulnerable — because both parties know the other can't walk away at low cost. The burned ship is what makes the voyage possible.

Of course, this cuts both ways. A commitment device is a weapon if misused. People trapped in harmful marriages by the very structural constraints that were supposed to enable trust. The lesson: commitment devices are powerful precisely because they're hard to reverse — which means choosing them wisely is one of the highest-stakes decisions you'll make.
contrast.title: The Contrast

principle.content:
A commitment device is any action that deliberately restricts your future options to make a promise credible. It works by changing the payoff structure: after the device is activated, breaking the commitment becomes more costly than keeping it.

Three conditions make commitment devices work:
1. **Irreversibility** — the option must be genuinely destroyed, not just made difficult
2. **Visibility** — others must be able to see that the commitment is structural, not just verbal
3. **Costliness** — the device must be expensive enough that only a sincere person would activate it (connecting back to costly signaling, mission 114)

Promises backed only by willpower fail because willpower fluctuates. Promises backed by structural constraint succeed because the structure doesn't fluctuate. The art is choosing which futures to destroy.

psychology.content:
Commitment devices exploit a well-documented feature of human psychology: temporal discounting. We systematically overvalue present rewards and undervalue future costs. Your present self genuinely wants to wake at 5am; your future self (at 5am) genuinely prefers sleep. Without a structural constraint, the future self always wins because it's the one making the actual decision.

Behavioral economist Richard Thaler documented how people use commitment devices intuitively: putting money in Christmas savings accounts that pay lower interest (making early withdrawal costly), joining gyms with annual contracts (making quitting expensive), or telling friends about a goal (making failure publicly embarrassing).

The neuroscience confirms the split: the prefrontal cortex makes the commitment ("I'll exercise daily") but the limbic system makes the in-the-moment decision ("I'll start tomorrow"). Commitment devices work by making the prefrontal cortex's decision structurally binding on the limbic system's future choices.

Research by Ariely and Wertenbroch (2002) showed that students who set binding deadlines for themselves (with real penalties for missing them) significantly outperformed those with flexible deadlines — even though the flexible-deadline students could have set the same schedule voluntarily. The binding constraint, not the intention, produced the behavior.

This has profound implications for organizational design: policies, contracts, and systems that create structural commitment outperform those that rely on motivation, culture, or good intentions. The question isn't "are people motivated?" It's "is breaking the commitment more expensive than keeping it?"
psychology.title: Why It Happens

move.content:
1. Make promises structural, not verbal. When you commit to something — a goal, a relationship, a deal — ask: what would make breaking this physically, financially, or socially expensive? Then activate that mechanism. Tell your team publicly. Put money at stake. Sign a contract. Destroy your exit.

2. Identify your Sirens before you hear them. Odysseus tied himself to the mast *before* the temptation arrived. Design your commitment devices in moments of clarity, not moments of weakness. What future situations will your future self rationalize escaping from? Pre-commit now.

3. Use commitment devices to make others' promises credible. In negotiations, don't trust verbal promises. Ask: what structure makes breaking this commitment costly for them? Deposits, penalties, public announcements, phased delivery with payment tied to milestones — these are commitment devices that make promises believable.

4. Match the device to the stakes. Small commitments (exercise, studying) need small devices (public accountability, loss of a small deposit). Large commitments (partnerships, career changes) need large devices (legal contracts, financial investment, burned alternatives). Over-committing to small things is as dangerous as under-committing to large ones.

5. Respect the irreversibility. Commitment devices work because they're hard to undo. This means choosing poorly is expensive. Before burning ships, be sure you want to reach the other shore. Before marrying, be sure this is the person. The power of commitment devices is exactly proportional to the danger of activating them carelessly.
